_meta.usage_guide: Select persona by: personas[ethnicity].age_groups[age_group_key]. Age group keys: gen_z (born 1997-2012, age ~13-28), millennial (born 1981-1996, age ~29-44), gen_x (born 1965-1980, age ~45-60), baby_boomer (born 1946-1964, age ~61-79), silent_gen (born before 1946, age 80+). The base_persona always applies; age_group OVERRIDES or EXTENDS fields when more specific behavior is needed.

_nation_notes.sensitivities: Multi-ethnic nation (Malay ~69%, Chinese ~23%, Indian ~7%) with Islam as the state religion. The Sedition Act 1948 and Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 restrict content that could cause racial or religious disharmony. Content must respect the Rukunegara principles and the constitutional position of the Malay rulers. Racial and religious harmony is a foundational national value — any content perceived as divisive is treated with extreme severity.
_nation_notes.language_complexity: Bahasa Melayu is the national language and must be used in all official contexts. English is widely used in business and urban settings. Chinese (Mandarin, Cantonese, Hokkien) and Tamil are community languages. Code-switching between languages (Manglish, Rojak) is common in informal contexts but must be used authentically, not mockingly.
_nation_notes.digital_landscape: Very high smartphone penetration (~97%). Dominant platforms: TikTok, Instagram, Facebook, WhatsApp. E-wallets (Touch 'n Go eWallet, GrabPay, Boost) widely adopted. Strong social commerce via Shopee and TikTok Shop. Astro remains dominant for TV/streaming.
_nation_notes.general_modesty: Conservative dress standards apply across all ethnicities in public-facing advertising. Avoid showing excessive skin (armpits, midriff, thighs), intimate body areas, or suggestive poses regardless of target ethnicity. This is especially critical for Malay audiences but applies as a general national baseline.
_nation_notes.food_sensitivity: Malaysia has strict halal certification governed by JAKIM (Department of Islamic Development Malaysia). Non-halal food/drink must be clearly separated in advertising. During Ramadan, food advertising timing and imagery must be sensitive. Pork and alcohol must never appear in the same frame as halal products.
_nation_notes.religious_diversity: While Islam is the state religion, Malaysia is home to significant Buddhist, Hindu, Christian, and Taoist communities. Advertising that targets 'all' Malaysians must be respectful of all faiths and avoid privileging or marginalizing any single religious group.
_nation_notes.political_sensitivity: Avoid references to politically sensitive topics including race-based policies (NEP/DEB), political parties, or the royal institution (unless in a respectful, ceremonial context). Satirizing government officials or policies in commercial advertising is high-risk.

demographics.primary_language: Bahasa Melayu, English (educated urban), mix of both
demographics.key_traits: Community-oriented, deeply Islamic, respectful of hierarchy, warm hospitality (mesra), values family and gotong-royong (communal cooperation)

malay.historical_and_cultural_context: Malay culture in Malaysia is deeply intertwined with Islamic principles. Advertising must respect both traditional Malay cultural norms (kesopanan/politeness) and strict Islamic jurisprudence (Shariah). The community is united by shared faith, language, and a strong sense of national identity tied to the concept of 'Ketuanan Melayu' and Rukun Negara values.
core_values: Respect for religious sanctity and Islamic principles
Filial piety and respect for elders (menghormati ibu bapa)
strict_taboos: NEVER show or mention pork, alcohol, or non-halal ingredients (e.g., pig-derived collagen).
NEVER show women's hair uncovered (without hijab).
NEVER show arms above the elbow or legs above the knee.
NEVER show bare armpits (even for deodorant ads) or bare male torsos.
NEVER use Islamic symbols (Mosques, Quran verses, Arabic calligraphy) as commercial props or placed disrespectfully (e.g., on floors/footwear).
NEVER show people eating or drinking during daylight in Ramadan-themed ads.
NEVER mock or diminish the Malay language, royalty, or national identity.
NEVER show non-mahram physical contact between opposite genders.
nuances_and_behavior: Physical contact (hugging, hand-holding) between non-related males and females (non-mahram) is highly inappropriate.
Romantic scenes must remain strictly within publicly acceptable limits and ideally depict married couples.
Sensual dancing, hip movements, provocative poses, and suggestive body movements are offensive regardless of product category.
Applying products to intimate body areas (underarms, thighs) must not be shown directly.
Ramadan and Hari Raya are peak emotional and commercial periods — authenticity and sincerity are paramount.
Local Malay slang (e.g., 'lah', 'kan', 'wehh') builds rapport but must be age-appropriate.

gen_z.label: Malay Gen Z — The Digital Ummah
gen_z.tone_and_language: Casual Bahasa Melayu mixed with English (Manglish), heavy use of internet slang (e.g., 'boleh lagi ke?', 'fuh', 'gempak'), TikTok-native humor, meme culture
key_motivations: Halal lifestyle content (halal skincare, fashion, food trends)
Islamic identity that feels modern, cool, and globally connected
Social justice and environmental awareness through Islamic lens
gen_z.content_style: Short-form video, memes, authentic UGC (User Generated Content), micro-influencer endorsements, relatable everyday scenarios
gen_z.brand_trust_signals: Muslim-owned brands, halal certification prominently displayed, micro-influencer authenticity over celebrity endorsements, peer reviews on TikTok/Shopee
gen_z.spending_behavior: Value-conscious, influenced by social commerce (TikTok Shop, Shopee Live), side-gig income, digital wallets (GrabPay, Touch 'n Go eWallet)

overrides.communication_style: Peer-to-peer, highly informal, humor and meme-driven
overrides.media_consumption: Exclusively digital-first; newspaper and TV are near-irrelevant
overrides.religious_expression: Islam is a personal identity badge — proudly visible (hijab fashion, halal lifestyle branding) but not preachy

millennial.label: Malay Millennial — The Aspirational Muslim Professional
millennial.tone_and_language: Bilingual (Bahasa Melayu + English), professional yet warm, code-switching is natural
key_motivations: Giving children the best Islamic education
Social status through lifestyle (cars, gadgets, home décor)
millennial.content_style: Aspirational lifestyle content, family-centric narratives, Islamic values woven naturally, testimonial-driven, Raya/Ramadan themed campaigns
millennial.brand_trust_signals: Trusted local and regional brands, government-backed halal certification (JAKIM), word-of-mouth in WhatsApp family/friend groups, thought-leader endorsements
millennial.spending_behavior: Highest spending power in the Malay demographic, purchase-driven by life milestones (marriage, children, home), balance between Islamic products and global mainstream brands

overrides.communication_style: Aspirational but grounded, family storytelling resonates deeply
overrides.media_consumption: Omnichannel — active on both digital and traditional (Astro, Malay drama streaming)

gen_x.label: Malay Gen X — The Established Provider
gen_x.tone_and_language: Formal-leaning Bahasa Melayu, respect for proper grammar, cautious with English-heavy content
key_motivations: Financial security and retirement planning (KWSP/EPF awareness)
Health and wellness (aging parents, personal health)
gen_x.content_style: Informative, factual, respectful tone, longer-form content acceptable, endorsements by respected community figures or Islamic scholars (ustaz)
gen_x.brand_trust_signals: Established brand history, government affiliation or endorsement, Islamic scholarly approval, community recommendations
gen_x.spending_behavior: Brand loyal, less experimental, high investment in insurance, takaful, education, and health products

overrides.communication_style: Formal and respectful, avoid slang or overly casual tone
overrides.media_consumption: TV (Astro Oasis, TV3) and Facebook are dominant; TikTok only for watching grandchildren's content

baby_boomer.label: Malay Baby Boomer — The Merdeka Generation
baby_boomer.tone_and_language: Formal Bahasa Melayu, classical expressions, deep respect for authority and proper language
key_motivations: Health products and medications (diabetes, hypertension are prevalent)
Traditional remedies (herbal, Malay traditional medicine — ubat kampung)
baby_boomer.content_style: Simple, clear, large text, authoritative voice, religious framing, health-focused testimonials from peers
baby_boomer.brand_trust_signals: Government endorsement, doctor/health professional recommendation, long-established brand heritage, religious figure approval
baby_boomer.spending_behavior: Fixed income (pension/EPF), highly price-sensitive, prefers proven products, very brand loyal, influenced by family members for digital purchases

overrides.communication_style: Simple, authoritative, use of classic Malay proverbs (peribahasa) builds emotional resonance
overrides.digital_literacy: Low-to-moderate; WhatsApp is primary digital touchpoint, often rely on children to navigate apps

silent_gen.label: Malay Silent Generation — The Patriarchs
silent_gen.tone_and_language: Classical Malay, highly formal, deeply religious framing
key_motivations: Family legacy and spiritual guidance role
silent_gen.content_style: Extremely simple, large visuals, religious messaging, no digital complexity
silent_gen.brand_trust_signals: Doctor or imam recommendation, family trust, nationally recognized brands

overrides.digital_literacy: Very low; essentially offline — reach through family intermediaries or traditional broadcast media

demographics.primary_language: Mandarin, English, Cantonese (in KL/Selangor), Hokkien (in Penang/Northern states), Hakka
demographics.key_traits: Pragmatic, education-driven, business-oriented, strong community bonds, superstition-aware, multilingual, filial piety

chinese.historical_and_cultural_context: Malaysian Chinese culture is shaped by traditional Chinese beliefs, including Confucian respect for elders, Taoist/Buddhist spiritual practices, and deep-seated beliefs in numerology and symbolism. Unlike Singaporean Chinese who are predominantly English-educated, Malaysian Chinese retain strong vernacular roots through Chinese-medium education (SRJKCs), giving them a distinct Mandarin/dialect-first identity.
core_values: Respect for elders and ancestors (filial piety — xiao)
Education as the primary path to success
Superstitions surrounding gift-giving, death, and numbers
strict_taboos: NEVER use the number 4 (sounds like death) in pricing, quantities, or phone numbers. 14 and 44 are extremely inauspicious.
NEVER show clocks, broken mirrors, scissors, or shoes as gifts (associated with funerals, broken relationships, or bad luck).
NEVER show umbrellas opened indoors.
NEVER show chopsticks stuck vertically in rice (resembles funeral incense).
NEVER use ancestral tablets, altars, or Qing Ming (Tomb Sweeping) items (joss paper, incense) as commercial props.
NEVER use Buddhist monks or Taoist priests to endorse commercial products.
NEVER depict elderly Chinese in foolish, undignified, or helpless situations.
nuances_and_behavior: White is associated with mourning — avoid all-white packaging or backgrounds for celebratory occasions.
Black combined with white evokes funeral imagery.
Red signifies prosperity and is highly encouraged for festive contexts (like Chinese New Year).
During the Nine Emperor Gods vegetarian festival, meat product advertisements should be scheduled sensitively.
The number 8 (sounds like 'prosper') and 6 (smooth/prosperous) are highly auspicious in pricing and branding.
CNY gifting culture is enormous — hampers, premium packaging, and gold imagery drive purchase decisions.

gen_z.label: Malaysian Chinese Gen Z — The Digitally Bicultural
gen_z.tone_and_language: English-Mandarin-Cantonese mix (Manglish with Chinese code-switching), heavy internet slang, Chinese meme culture (from mainland China and local), streetwear and K-pop influenced vocabulary
key_motivations: Global-local identity (proud of Chinese heritage but also Malaysian)
Social media clout and creator culture
preferred_channels: XiaoHongShu (RED app — especially for Chinese-educated segment)
gen_z.content_style: Short-form video, bilingual content (Mandarin + English captions), aesthetic product shoots, 'day in the life' content, humor that blends Chinese and Malaysian cultural references
gen_z.brand_trust_signals: Peer reviews on Shopee/Lazada, KOL endorsements, authentic UGC, Chinese brand crossovers (local brands adopting CNY aesthetics year-round), transparent ingredient lists
gen_z.spending_behavior: Digitally-driven purchases (TikTok Shop, Shopee Live), influenced by trending aesthetics, participates in group buys, uses digital wallets

overrides.communication_style: Peers-only tone; celebrity advertising feels inauthentic — micro-influencers and relatable content win
overrides.cultural_identity: Blend of local Malaysian Chinese pride + global Chinese pop culture (C-pop, Chinese drama); distinct from mainland Chinese identity
overrides.superstition_relevance: Lower adherence to strict superstitions vs. older generations; however, CNY aesthetics and lucky numbers still organically resonate

millennial.label: Malaysian Chinese Millennial — The Pragmatic Achiever
millennial.tone_and_language: English-dominant in professional/urban contexts, Mandarin or Cantonese in family and community contexts, Manglish in casual social settings
key_motivations: Children's education (high spend on tuition, enrichment classes)
Health and wellness (gym, organic food, supplements)
Work-life balance and experience economy (travel, dining)
millennial.content_style: Aspirational lifestyle, family milestones, product quality demonstrations, comparison content, testimonials from credible peers
millennial.brand_trust_signals: Established brand with quality track record, strong online reviews, recommendations from peer networks, premium packaging, awards and certifications
millennial.spending_behavior: Highest disposable income bracket of the Chinese demographic, brand-loyal for core products but willing to try premium alternatives, heavy spend on children, property, and experiences

overrides.communication_style: Blend of aspirational and practical; quality and value must co-exist
overrides.superstition_relevance: CNY and major festivals still drive big purchases; auspicious numbers in pricing work well for high-ticket items

gen_x.label: Malaysian Chinese Gen X — The Established Business Class
gen_x.tone_and_language: Cantonese or Mandarin in personal/community settings, English in business, reads both English and Chinese press
key_motivations: Health investments (preventive healthcare, supplements, medical insurance)
High-value discretionary purchases (cars, luxury goods, premium travel)
gen_x.content_style: Factual, quality-demonstrating, status-affirming, heritage and craftsmanship narratives, family legacy stories
gen_x.brand_trust_signals: Long brand heritage, word-of-mouth from peers in business networks, Chinese newspaper features, awards and industry recognition
gen_x.spending_behavior: High purchasing power, brand loyal but quality-driven; willing to pay premium for proven products; heavily influenced by community business networks (associations, guilds)

overrides.communication_style: Formal-to-moderate tone; respect for expertise and knowledge; avoid overly casual or slang-heavy communication
overrides.superstition_relevance: High; especially for business decisions — auspicious dates for launches, lucky numbers in pricing, feng shui considerations

baby_boomer.label: Malaysian Chinese Baby Boomer — The Merdeka Pragmatist
baby_boomer.tone_and_language: Chinese dialects (Cantonese, Hokkien, Hakka) in daily life, Mandarin in formal Chinese contexts, limited English
key_motivations: Health maintenance (Traditional Chinese Medicine, supplements, hospitals)
Community association participation (clan associations, temple activities)
preferred_channels: Chinese newspapers (Sin Chew Daily, Nanyang Siang Pau)
baby_boomer.content_style: Simple, health-focused, family-oriented narratives, traditional values affirmed, authoritative health expert testimonials in Mandarin/Cantonese
baby_boomer.brand_trust_signals: Doctor/sinseh (TCM practitioner) recommendation, long-established Chinese brand names, peers in clan/community associations, visible product efficacy
baby_boomer.spending_behavior: Fixed or pension income, price-sensitive, trusts established Chinese brands deeply, high spend on health products and grandchildren's gifts, CNY spending is significant

overrides.communication_style: Speak in dialects if possible; Mandarin is respectful; English-only alienates
overrides.superstition_relevance: Very high — especially health superstitions, auspicious timing, and ancestral rituals during festivals
overrides.digital_literacy: Low-moderate; WhatsApp and YouTube are primary digital touchpoints; navigated with family assistance

silent_gen.label: Malaysian Chinese Silent Generation — The Clan Elders
silent_gen.tone_and_language: Dialects (Cantonese/Hokkien/Hakka), very little English, classical Mandarin
silent_gen.content_style: Simple visuals, classical Chinese motifs, health and longevity themes
silent_gen.brand_trust_signals: Family recommendation, clan association trust, long-established brand

overrides.digital_literacy: Very low — reach through family intermediaries or traditional Chinese broadcast media
overrides.communication_style: Dialects preferred; any content must be visually simple and text-minimal

demographics.religion: Predominantly Hindu (~85%), with Tamil Christian and Muslim minorities
demographics.key_traits: Family-centric, festival-driven, value-conscious, deeply spiritual, community-solidarity (especially Tamil community), resilient economic climbers

indian.historical_and_cultural_context: Malaysian Indian culture is heavily influenced by Hindu religious traditions, requiring deep reverence for sacred symbols, respect for family hierarchy, and adherence to specific dietary restrictions. The community — predominantly Tamil — traces its history to plantation labor during British colonial rule and has since built strong professional and entrepreneurial communities. Tamil media (cinema, music) is a dominant cultural force across all age groups.
core_values: Reverence for the divine and sacred symbols
Respect for family hierarchy and elders
Tamil cultural pride — language, cinema, music
Communal solidarity (koodum vizhavum — gathering and festivals)
strict_taboos: NEVER show beef or beef-derived products (including explicit cow-leather). The cow is sacred.
NEVER place Hindu deities or sacred symbols (Om, Swastika, Trishul, Lotus) on footwear, floor materials, or below waist level.
NEVER use sacred symbols or deities to sell alcohol or meat.
NEVER use Hindu deity imagery (e.g., Ganesha) as cartoon characters or playful mascots for children's products.
NEVER show the sacred lamp (vilakku/deepam) extinguished or used merely as a decorative prop.
NEVER depict occupational stereotyping based on caste associations or untouchability concepts.
NEVER use Deepavali or Tamil New Year imagery as generic 'decoration' without cultural authenticity.
nuances_and_behavior: During major Hindu festivals (Navratri, Maha Shivaratri, Thaipusam), non-vegetarian food should not be prominently featured.
Saffron (kesari) represents sacrifice/purity and must not be used frivolously.
White represents mourning — avoid all-white attire on Indian models in celebratory contexts.
Vermillion (sindoor) and married woman symbols (mangalsutra, toe rings, bindi) must not be commercialized as mere fashion accessories.
Traditional attire (saree, salwar kameez, veshti) must be depicted respectfully without sexualization.
Tamil cinema references and Tamil music/BGM in ads resonate deeply as cultural shorthand.

gen_z.label: Malaysian Indian Gen Z — The Tamil Digital Native
gen_z.tone_and_language: Tanglish (Tamil-English mix), Manglish, meme references from Tamil cinema (especially Thalapathy Vijay, Rajinikanth meme culture), casual and peer-driven
key_motivations: Tamil cultural pride with modern twist (Tamil music, K-drama crossover, Tamil memes)
Social media presence and creator economy
Educational content (competitive exam prep content, skill-building)
gen_z.content_style: Tamil-English mixed short videos, Tamil cinema BGM in ads, meme-driven content, festival content with modern aesthetic, relatable everyday Tamil Malaysian life scenarios
gen_z.brand_trust_signals: Tamil language content, community-relevant social causes, local Indian influencers, peer-reviewed products on social platforms, Deepavali campaign authenticity
gen_z.spending_behavior: Budget-conscious, strong festival (Deepavali) spending spikes, influenced by Tamil social media trends, prefers social commerce and digital wallets

overrides.communication_style: Casual Tamil-English mix; meme culture and Tamil cinema references are powerful engagement tools
overrides.religious_expression: Hinduism is culturally celebrated but worn lightly in daily digital life — festivals are key activation moments
overrides.representation_need: HIGH — Malaysian Indian community is underrepresented in mainstream ads; authentic Tamil representation drives very strong brand affinity

millennial.label: Malaysian Indian Millennial — The Aspiring Professional
millennial.tone_and_language: English-dominant professionally, Tamil in family and community contexts, code-switching between English and Tamil naturally
key_motivations: Deepavali celebrations — significant gifting and clothing spend
millennial.content_style: Family-centric, aspirational, Tamil cultural themes, festival season campaigns, health and education narratives, relatable middle-class Indian Malaysian stories
millennial.brand_trust_signals: Tamil community endorsements, doctor/health professional recommendations, strong Deepavali campaign associations, brands that support Tamil community causes
millennial.spending_behavior: Growing disposable income, highest spend during Deepavali and wedding seasons, brand-loyal for trusted products, invests significantly in children's education

overrides.communication_style: Balance between Tamil cultural pride and modern professional aspirations; avoid patronizing or stereotyped 'plantation-era' imagery

gen_x.label: Malaysian Indian Gen X — The Community Pillar
gen_x.tone_and_language: Tamil in home and community, English in professional settings, formal register
key_motivations: Health and wellness (aging concerns, preventive healthcare)
Social status within the Indian community
gen_x.content_style: Traditional values affirmed, health expert testimonials in Tamil, family and community themes, religious festival anchoring, long-form storytelling acceptable
gen_x.brand_trust_signals: Tamil community leaders, religious authority figures, established brand with long Indian community association, doctor-recommended for health products
gen_x.spending_behavior: Stable income, high commitment to children's education, significant temple and community donations, loyal to brands that support Tamil community

overrides.communication_style: Formal Tamil is highly respected; reference to traditional Tamil values (kaaval, katrupadi — discipline) resonates
overrides.superstition_relevance: High; auspicious timing for purchases, horoscope considerations for major life decisions

baby_boomer.label: Malaysian Indian Baby Boomer — The Temple Generation
baby_boomer.tone_and_language: Predominantly Tamil, limited English, formal and traditional register
key_motivations: Religious devotion and temple activities (Thaipusam kavadi, daily pooja)
Traditional Ayurvedic and herbal health remedies
baby_boomer.content_style: Simple Tamil language, health-focused, religious framing, traditional Indian imagery (temple festivals, classical attire), authority figure testimonials
baby_boomer.brand_trust_signals: Temple priest or community leader endorsement, traditionally used remedies, family generational trust in a brand
baby_boomer.spending_behavior: Fixed income, price-sensitive, high spend on temple donations and festival preparations, deeply loyal to brands used across family generations

overrides.communication_style: Pure Tamil; any English-heavy content is alienating
overrides.digital_literacy: Low; WhatsApp is primary digital touchpoint, TV and radio remain dominant media

silent_gen.label: Malaysian Indian Silent Generation — The Estate Elders
silent_gen.tone_and_language: Classical Tamil dialect, very little English or Bahasa Malaysia
silent_gen.content_style: Simple Tamil visuals, devotional themes, traditional motifs
silent_gen.brand_trust_signals: Family and temple community recommendation only

overrides.digital_literacy: Near zero — exclusively reached through family intermediaries or Tamil broadcast media
